segments.text: Happy Friday, everyone, and welcome to episode seven of Modern Investing Techniques.
I'm Patrick, coming to you from Vancouver on this last trading day of the week, March 27th,
2026. Over the past five sessions, we've seen some of the sharpest moves driven by geopolitical
headlines in quite a while, so today we're going to do a proper weekly review, talk about how to filter
out the noise, review our simulated practice position, look at platform tools that actually help in
these environments, and leave you with practical steps you can use in your own TFSA or RRSP next week.
Before we dive in, the usual important reminder, everything we discuss on this show is strictly
for education and entertainment purposes. The practice investment of the day uses completely
simulated trades with zero real money at risk. I am not a licensed financial advisor,
and nothing you hear here constitutes financial advice, investment recommendations,
or a solicitation to buy or sell any security. Markets involve real risk of loss,
always do your own research and consult a qualified financial advisor before putting any of your
hard-earned capital to work. Pass performance, even in our simulated account, does not predict future
results. Let's start with the market action today. Oil prices climbed noticeably after reports
that President Trump noted Iran had allowed 10 tankers to pass through the Strait of Hormuz,
that single piece of news injected fresh volatility into the entire energy sector.
At the same time, North American equities opened lower and stayed under pressure for most of the
session. The S&P 500 finished the day at 6477 down 1.7% on the session. The NASDAQ composite was
hit harder, closing at 21,408 off 2.4%. Here in Canada, the TSX composite closed at 31,888
down 1.5%. What we're seeing is a classic divergence. Oil moving higher on geopolitical supply
concerns while broader equities remain under pressure from risk off sentiment. The Iran conflict is
now approaching the one-month mark, and every new headline seems to create another wave of sentiment swings.
If you hold energy names inside your TFSA or RRSP, this is definitely worth watching closely,
but Canadian investors should be careful not to let daily oil price swings override long-term
fundamental analysis. Chasing short-term commodity moves inside tax-advantaged accounts has burned
many retail investors over the years. While today's headlines are dominated by conflict-driven
volatility, let's take a moment to look at a disciplined framework that can help you cut through
the noise in exactly these kinds of environments. I call it beyond the headline geopolitical filtering.
The core idea is to deliberately separate short-term noise, things like sudden oil spikes,
tanker headlines, or war rhetoric, from the long-term fundamental value of the businesses you own.
When you see a sector or individual stock jump or drop 3% or more in a single day purely on geopolitical
news, the disciplined move is to pause before you act. Pull up three specific metrics, the company's
free cash flow yield, its revenue growth trends over the past several quarters, and a clear understanding
of its competitive moat. You can find this information quickly on seeking alpha, your broker's research
tab, or even the company's latest investor presentation. This simple pause and check habit keeps you
from reacting purely to price action and headlines. Historically, this approach has served investors
well during prolonged conflicts. Think back to the Gulf War period or the early stages of the
Ukraine invasion. Initial oil spikes and equity sell-offs often faded within four to eight weeks,
while companies with strong balance sheets and real earnings power continued to compound.
The risk, of course, is filtering too aggressively and missing a genuine structural supply shift that
could last years rather than weeks. For most Canadian investors managing a TFSA or RRSP,
the practical step is straightforward. The next time you see that 3% or larger geopolitical move
force yourself to spend at least five minutes checking those three metrics before you adjust
any position. Over time, this single habit can dramatically improve your decision quality during
uncertain periods. This filtering approach becomes even more critical when liquidity starts to dry up
because that's when hidden costs can quietly destroy returns. Let me paint a realistic picture
that actually happened to many traders last week. Imagine you placed a market order to buy an energy
ETF the moment the first major Iran headlines hit. Your order filled at $42.15, but when you looked
at the closing price that same day, the ETF finished at only $41.98. The bid-ask spread had blown out
from its normal three cents to $0.22 within the first 45 minutes of trading. Market makers pulled
liquidity fast amid the uncertainty and that invisible slippage cost you roughly $50 on a $10.000
position. During geopolitical stress, spreads on many TSX-listed energy names can jump forward to
seven times their normal levels. Professional traders always check both the bid-ask spread and the
order book depth before hitting the market order button. This is especially true in the first 90 minutes
after major news breaks. The biggest mistake retail investors make is assuming the last quoted price
they see is the price they will actually get. Instead, use limit orders set somewhere between the
current bid and ask when spreads are wider than 10 cents or temporarily switched to larger, more
liquid ETFs during these volatile stretches. These are small habits that protect capital when emotions
are running high. With those costly but valuable lessons in mind, let's move to our practice investment
of the day. Remember, this is a simulated trade used purely for educational purposes. No real money
is involved. This week, this weekly hold is Western Digital Corp, WDC, on the NASDAQ. The strategy here
is a fundamental growth play centered on cloud-driven revenue and margin expansion inside a still
volatile memory sector. Recent analysis has clearly identified cloud spending as the primary driver
for both top-line growth and margin improvement at Western Digital. Technically, the stock is currently
trading near its 50-day moving average after a recent dip, which offers a reasonable risk reward setup
for a weekly hold. We'll be watching for volume confirmation, specifically volume moving above
the 20-day average, on any bounce from that support area. On the risk side, further escalation in
the Middle East conflict could pressure overall technology spending and delay corporate cloud budgets.
To manage that risk, we have a mental stop loss set at 6% below our entry point to limit drawdown
on the simulated position. Our target range for this weekly hold is between 4% and 9% by Friday's
close. I'm assigning this idea a medium confidence level. The cloud growth narrative gives us a clear
fundamental tailwind, but sector-wide memory weakness combined with ongoing geopolitical risk
creates real offsetting uncertainty. The purpose of featuring this simulated position is to demonstrate
how to anchor a weekly hold in a specific fundamental driver rather than chasing pure price momentum.
It teaches you to separate company-specific positives from macro noise. Regardless of how
this trade ultimately plays out, the discipline of defining both an exit range and a maximum loss
before you enter any position is one of the best ways to protect capital inside your TFSA or RRSP
over the long term. We'll track this simulated position through next week and evaluate it on Friday's
show before we close out the week. Let's quickly review yesterday's simulated trade result so we can
learn from it in real time. Yesterday's weekly hold was SoFi technologies. SoFi. We entered the simulated
position at $17.16 on Monday's open. By the end of the week, it had closed at $15.00
in 87 cents, producing a loss of 7.49%. On a simulated $1,000 position that equals a loss of $74.91.
Our running total across the two simulated trades now stands at minus $74.91. The win rate sits at
zero wins out of two trades. The short interest thesis we were following simply did not overcome the
broader market pressure last week. This outcome highlights the importance of requiring both elevated
short interest and a clear technical catalyst before taking mean reversion setups. Examples of that
catalyst would be an RSI reading below 30 or a positive volume inflection on the daily chart.
We will be tightening those combined criteria for any future setups of this type. Even with these
early simulated losses, I remain convinced that modern tools and discipline processes can still give
individual investors a real edge. Speaking of tools, let's look at two platforms that I believe are
especially useful right now. First is Seeking Alpha Premium. The depth of its article library and
earnings call transcript collection allows you to filter specifically by themes such as cloud growth
or memory stocks. You also get access to full earnings call presentations and detailed analyst
deep dives that often surface fundamental drivers months before they become consensus. This is
particularly useful when geopolitical noise is dominating the headlines. The free tier offers limited
access but premium costs approximately $239 per year and unlocks unlimited articles, transcripts,
and their proprietary quant ratings. It's an excellent resource for anyone managing their own TFSA
or RRSP who wants to move beyond simple index fund investing. The second platform worth considering
is interactive brokers portfolio analyst tool. Their free geopolitical overlay feature now lets you
stress test your entire portfolio against various oil price scenarios and regional conflict variables.
You can upload your TFSA or RRSP holdings and instantly see estimated correlations and drawdown
projections under different Iran related outcomes. This help as you quantify exactly how much energy
or technology exposure you can comfortably carry without losing sleep. Best of all it's available
at no extra cost to IBKR clients and works on both web and mobile platforms. I have no financial
relationship with either of these companies. I simply find them genuinely useful in the current
environment. Finally, here are four quick hits that caught my attention today and deserve a bit more
context. JP Morgan recently highlighted significant upside potential in European defense stocks and
flagged its top pick in the sector. For globally diversified Canadian investors, this could
represent a sensible sector rotation opportunity outside of North American markets. Reviewing European
defense names that also sports strong balance sheets may act as a partial hedge against prolonged
geopolitical tension. Citizens financial reiterated its positive rating on Shopify, citing continued
momentum in the enterprise segment. This is encouraging for Canadian investors because Shopify remains
one of the few genuine Canadian growth compounders. It suggests the company is still a core holding
candidate for growth-oriented TFSA portfolios even while broader markets are weak. Taiwan made
the notable decision to keep power prices unchanged despite the Middle East conflict. That move removes
a key cost risk for global semiconductor supply chains and supports taking a more measured approach
to memory and chip stocks rather than panic selling on every new Iran headline. Finally, Bank of America
expects further US dollar strength through the second quarter. For Canadian investors, this is a
reminder that keeping some USD cash or USD-denominated assets inside RRSP or FHSA accounts could help
hedge against further loony weakness if that forecast materializes. Let's take a moment to look at the
overall simulated portfolio performance. We have now completed two weekly holds. The win rate stands
at 0% with zero wins, one loss, and one break even trade. Cumulative profit and loss sits at
minus $74.91. The average return per trade is negative 3.75% and our current streak is one loss.
Our AI analysis is actively learning from these patterns and adjusting the entry criteria accordingly.
This remains a learning journey not a track record to follow blindly. The goal is to help you develop
better processes, not to copy any specific trade. Before we wrap up, keep a close eye on how cloud
related names hold up into next week given the ongoing geopolitical backdrop. That sector strength
or lack of it will tell us a lot about whether the market is differentiating between short-term
noise and longer-term structural growth. That's it. For today's episode of modern investing techniques,
you'll find links to all the resources, platforms, and metrics we discussed on the resources page
at the website. If you're finding value in these episodes, please subscribe, leave a review,
and share the show with someone else who wants to go beyond simple index fund investing. Thank you
for spending part of your Friday with me. Stay disciplined, stay patient, and I'll see you back
here on Monday, end of episode. This podcast is curated by Patrick but generated using AI voice
synthesis of my voice using 11 labs. The primary reason to do this is I unfortunately don't have the
time to be consistent with generating all the content and wanted to focus on creating consistent and
regular episodes for all the themes that I enjoy and I hope others do as well.
